citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The facility did not communicate timely and accurate information on all problems of animal health to the attending
veterinarian. The animals affected are as follows:
** Two young black and white goats were observed with limb issues. One of the goats had a clear limp on its front right
leg, but could stand and walk. The other goat was standing on three legs and holding up its rear right leg. It was reluctant
to move; the inspector did not observe the goat walking.
According to a facility representative, the goats' limb issues were identified in the past few days. The goat with the
forelimb issue had intermittent problems since he was born, but the representative did not observe a significant medical
concern until very recently. According to the attending veterinarian, a representative sent a text message to the
veterinarian about two goats with a "hoof problem" on Monday, June 1, two days prior to the inspection, and made an
appointment for Wednesday, June 10. The attending veterinarian was unaware of the severity of the problem and stated
that a goat that cannot bear weight on its leg should receive veterinary care sooner than 10 days after the problem was
identified.
** An adult white-tailed deer was observed with a poor coat and appeared very thin. The spine, scapula, and hips were
prominent and and the animal had unkempt and patchy fur loss on the back and sides. The attending veterinarian stated
that he examined the deer on May 2, 2026 (about 30 days before the inspection) and advised de-worming and monitoring
nutrition, with further diagnostics such as bloodwork and fecal testing if the animal did not improve. A facility
representative reported that the diarrhea improved, but the animal's body condition or fur condition did not significantly
improve. A facility representative has not communicated any updates on this animal's health since May 8, 2026, and has
not scheduled any follow-up diagnostics.
Failing to communicate timely and accurate information on problems of animal health and well-being to the attending
veterinarian has prevented the attending veterinarian from providing adequate veterinary care to these animals.
Correct by ensuring these animals are evaluated by a veterinarian and ensuring to communicate timely and accurate
information on problems of animal health and well-being to the attending veterinarian. Correction date: 6/5/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The facility did not communicate timely and accurate information on all problems of animal health to the attending 
veterinarian. The animals affected are as follows: 
 
** Two young black and white goats were observed with limb issues. One of the goats had a clear limp on its front right 
leg, but could stand and walk. The other goat was standing on three legs and holding up its rear right leg. It was reluctant 
to move; the inspector did not observe the goat walking.  
 According to a facility representative, the goats' limb issues were identified in the past few days. The goat with the 
forelimb issue had intermittent problems since he was born, but the representative did not observe a significant medical 
concern until very recently. According to the attending veterinarian, a representative sent a text message to the 
veterinarian about two goats with a "hoof problem" on Monday, June 1, two days prior to the inspection, and made an 
appointment for Wednesday, June 10. The attending veterinarian was unaware of the severity of the problem and stated 
that a goat that cannot bear weight on its leg should receive veterinary care sooner than 10 days after the problem was 
identified.  
 
** An adult white-tailed deer was observed with a poor coat and appeared very thin. The spine, scapula, and hips were 
prominent and and the animal had unkempt and patchy fur loss on the back and sides. The attending veterinarian stated 
that he examined the deer on May 2, 2026 (about 30 days before the inspection) and advised de-worming and monitoring 
nutrition, with further diagnostics such as bloodwork and fecal testing if the animal did not improve. A facility 
representative reported that the diarrhea improved, but the animal's body condition or fur condition did not significantly 
improve. A facility representative has not communicated any updates on this animal's health since May 8, 2026, and has 
not scheduled any follow-up diagnostics.  
 
Failing to communicate timely and accurate information on problems of animal health and well-being to the attending 
veterinarian has prevented the attending veterinarian from providing adequate veterinary care to these animals.  
 
Correct by ensuring these animals are evaluated by a veterinarian and ensuring to communicate timely and accurate 
information on problems of animal health and well-being to the attending veterinarian. Correction date: 6/5/2026.

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
